content.text: Every AI programme office eventually faces the same problem: more ideas than capacity.

When a financial-services firm receives forty-three AI initiative proposals in a single quarter, someone must decide which six enter the delivery pipeline, which nine get deferred, and which twenty-eight are declined — without destroying the credibility of the governance process.
Intake scoring criteria are the standardised, weighted rubrics that make that decision defensible, repeatable, and bias-resistant.

They reduce a multi-dimensional judgement call into a structured composite score that any committee member can challenge on the merits, any requester can appeal with evidence, and any auditor can inspect for fairness.
What It Is
Intake scoring criteria are a formally adopted set of
scoring dimensions
, each carrying a defined
weight
and a set of
sub-criteria
with anchored descriptors, that an intake committee applies to every proposed AI initiative before it enters the project portfolio.
The output is a single
composite score on a 0-100 scale
that places each proposal into one of four
decision bands
: auto-approve (>=80), committee review (60-79), defer (40-59), or reject (<40).
The framework has three structural components.
First, the
scoring rubric
— the YAML or structured document that defines every dimension, sub-criterion, weight, and descriptor.
Second, the
evaluation process
— who scores, when, how scores are aggregated, and how disagreements are resolved.
Third, the
decision and feedback loop
— what happens after scoring: communication to requesters, placement in the portfolio backlog, and the appeals mechanism.
Unlike ad-hoc prioritisation, intake scoring criteria are
defined before proposals arrive
, published to all potential requesters, and applied identically regardless of the proposer's seniority or political capital.
That pre-commitment is what gives the framework its governance value.
Why It Matters
Portfolio discipline at scale.

Without a shared rubric, each intake cycle degenerates into a negotiation between the loudest stakeholders. Scoring criteria externalise the decision logic, making it auditable and consistent across dozens of proposals per year.
Executive trust in the AI function.

When a CTO can see a ranked list with objective scores, they can defend resource allocation decisions to a board. Opacity invites second-guessing; a scoring matrix invites scrutiny — which builds long-term credibility.
Requester equity.

A junior product manager and a VP compete on the same rubric. Without criteria, political weight dominates. With criteria, a well-reasoned proposal from any level of the organisation can outrank a vague request with senior sponsorship.
Resource protection.

AI delivery teams are scarce and expensive. Admitting under-specified or low-value initiatives wastes capacity, demoralises engineers, and produces failed projects that damage AI programme reputation.
Regulatory defensibility.

In regulated industries — banking, insurance, healthcare — demonstrating that AI initiative selection was governed by documented, risk-adjusted criteria is increasingly required by supervisors and auditors.
Portfolio balance.

Scoring dimensions like strategic alignment and urgency, when weighted deliberately, prevent the portfolio from drifting toward low-risk, low-value optimisation work at the expense of transformational investments.
Benefits
Reduced cycle time.
A well-designed rubric with clear thresholds can cut intake decision time from weeks of committee back-and-forth to a single two-hour review meeting.
Higher proposal quality.
When requesters know they will be scored on data readiness and executive sponsorship, they prepare better proposals. The rubric functions as a forcing function for up-front due diligence.
Transparent ranking.
Every stakeholder sees the same ranked list with scores, not a committee black box. This transparency reduces the volume of post-decision lobbying.
Consistent across cycles.
Quarterly intake cycles produce comparable scores across cohorts because the rubric is stable. Year-over-year trend analysis becomes possible.
Appeals legitimacy.
When a proposal is declined, the requester receives a score breakdown. They know exactly which dimensions pulled the score below threshold, and can gather additional evidence for resubmission.
Bias mitigation.
Anchored descriptors — specific language tied to each score point — reduce inter-rater variability and make it harder for reviewers to inflate scores for favoured requesters.
Portfolio analytics.
Score distributions reveal systemic patterns: if most proposals score poorly on data readiness, the organisation has a data infrastructure problem, not just a proposal quality problem.
Trade-offs and Challenges
Gaming risk.

Once the rubric is published, sophisticated requesters will tailor proposals to score well rather than to describe their initiative accurately. Strategic inflation of sub-criteria scores is the most common failure mode.
Weight contention.

Agreeing on dimension weights is a political act. Business units that favour speed will argue for higher urgency weight; risk functions will advocate for risk weight. The weighting conversation must happen explicitly and be documented.
False precision.
A score of 73 vs 74 is not a meaningful difference. Treating the composite score as a continuous ranking rather than a band assignment misleads decision-makers into over-precise comparisons.
Rubric staleness.
A rubric calibrated for 2024 AI infrastructure may misvalue proposals in 2026. Weights and descriptors must be reviewed at least annually as technology capability and strategic priorities shift.
Assessment cost.
Thorough scoring against anchored descriptors takes effort — typically 1-2 hours per reviewer per proposal. For large intake volumes, this becomes meaningful overhead on senior staff time.
Threshold rigidity.

Hard thresholds can reject strategically important initiatives that score poorly due to early-stage uncertainty (low data readiness, no exec sponsor yet). Override mechanisms are necessary but must be logged to avoid eroding the framework.
Dimensional correlation.

Strategic alignment and business value often correlate highly. A highly aligned initiative almost always has high business value, which can artificially inflate composite scores for certain proposal types.
How It Works
The intake scoring process operates on a defined cadence — most enterprises run quarterly intake cycles, with an expedited track for time-sensitive regulatory or competitive drivers.
A proposal enters the intake queue when a requester submits a completed
intake request form

, typically through a ServiceNow or Jira portal. The form enforces minimum fields: initiative title, requester and sponsor identity, problem statement, expected business outcome, indicative effort estimate, and a data availability self-assessment.
Incomplete forms are returned to the requester before scoring begins, eliminating the overhead of evaluating under-specified requests.
Once a proposal passes the completeness gate, it is assigned to a pool of three to five
independent reviewers
drawn from the intake committee. Each reviewer scores the proposal independently against the rubric — without seeing other reviewers' scores — to avoid anchoring bias.
Scores are collected in the evaluation tool and aggregated using the
committee median
rather than the mean. Median aggregation suppresses outlier scores from reviewers who may have a vested interest in a proposal's outcome.

After aggregation, the intake committee convenes for a calibration session. Proposals whose composite scores land near a threshold boundary — within five points of the 80, 60, or 40 cutoffs — receive additional discussion.
The committee may confirm the automated band assignment or, with a documented rationale,
adjust the final band by one level
in either direction. Adjustments must be logged with the committee member's name and rationale.
This preserves the value of the rubric while allowing human judgement to handle genuine edge cases.
Requesters receive a
score notification letter

within five business days of the committee session. The letter states the composite score, the breakdown by dimension, the decision band, and — for deferred or rejected proposals — the specific criteria that pulled the score below threshold.
Requesters have ten business days to file an appeal with additional evidence.
Architecture and Design
The scoring framework has four architectural layers that must be designed coherently.
The
rubric layer

is the canonical definition of dimensions, weights, sub-criteria, and descriptors. It is stored as a version-controlled YAML file and published to a governance wiki. All scoring tools derive their configuration from this file.
The
submission layer
is the intake request form — the structured interface through which requesters propose initiatives. It enforces completeness checks and populates a proposal record in the portfolio management system.
The
evaluation layer

consists of the tooling reviewers use to assign sub-criterion scores, the aggregation engine that computes dimension and composite scores, and the calibration interface the committee uses during boundary-case review.
The
decision and portfolio layer
receives the final composite score and band, creates or updates a portfolio record (Epic in Jira, Demand in ServiceNow PPM), and triggers the notification workflow to the requester.
Dimension weights must sum to 100. Each dimension has a maximum raw score (typically 5, matching a 1-5 scale per sub-criterion).
The normalisation formula is:
contribution = (raw_average / max_raw) x weight
. Composite score = sum of contributions across all dimensions.
This ensures the composite score always lies on a 0-100 scale regardless of the number of sub-criteria per dimension.
When to Use It
Large AI portfolio backlogs.
When proposed initiatives exceed delivery capacity by 3:1 or more, informal prioritisation becomes unmanageable. A scoring framework is justified from around fifteen proposals per cycle onward.
Multiple competing business units.
When initiatives come from several organisational silos with no shared prioritisation authority, a neutral rubric provides the common language needed to compare dissimilar proposals.
Regulated industries.

Banking, insurance, healthcare, and utilities face supervisory expectations around AI governance. A documented scoring framework with audit trails is often a regulatory requirement or supervisory expectation.
Post-pilot maturity.

Organisations graduating from an AI pilot phase to a scaled delivery programme need intake discipline to avoid 'pilot purgatory' — dozens of successful pilots with no clear path to production prioritisation.
New AI programme setup.
At programme inception, establishing scoring criteria before the first proposals arrive sets the governance tone and prevents early political capture of the intake process.
Budget cycle alignment.
When AI initiative proposals need to be ranked to support annual or quarterly budget allocation decisions, a scoring framework provides the structured output finance teams need.
How to Implement
Convene a rubric design workshop.

Bring together representatives from strategy, finance, technology, risk, and one or two business unit heads. Agree on dimensions and weights before any specific proposal is on the table. Weight agreement made in the abstract is far more durable than weight agreement made while evaluating a live proposal.
Define dimensions and weights.

Start with the five canonical dimensions: strategic alignment (25), business value (25), technical feasibility (20), risk profile (15), urgency (15). Adjust weights to reflect your organisation's current strategic priorities.
Author sub-criteria and anchored descriptors.

For each dimension, define two to three sub-criteria. For each sub-criterion, write specific, unambiguous descriptor text for each score point (1 through 5). Vague descriptors are the leading cause of inter-rater inconsistency.
Encode the rubric in a version-controlled YAML file.
This becomes the system of record. Tag each rubric version with a semantic version number and effective date. All evaluation tooling loads the rubric from this file.
Build or configure the evaluation tool.

Options range from a structured spreadsheet with formula-driven scoring to a purpose-built Jira plugin or ServiceNow demand management workflow. The tool must enforce rubric structure and support multi-reviewer input.
Pilot with a historic cohort.

Before the first live intake cycle, score five to ten previously decided proposals using the new rubric. Compare rubric-derived decisions to actual historical decisions. Large discrepancies reveal weight calibration issues.
Publish the rubric and run a requester briefing.
Publish the complete rubric to your governance wiki. Run a thirty-minute briefing for potential requesters explaining each dimension, the scoring scale, the threshold bands, and the appeals process.
Run the first live intake cycle.
Score independently, aggregate by median, run the calibration session, issue notifications within five business days, and log every override or boundary adjustment.
Review and recalibrate annually.

After each calendar year, analyse the score distribution, inter-rater agreement statistics, and appeal outcomes. Adjust weights or descriptors if the rubric no longer reflects your strategic priorities.
The YAML configuration for the full rubric is shown below. This file is the source of truth for all evaluation tooling and portfolio system integrations.

# intake-scoring-rules.yaml  v2.1
# Source of truth for all intake evaluation tooling.

version: "2.1"
cycle: quarterly

dimensions:
  strategic_alignment:
    weight: 25
    max_raw: 5
    sub_criteria:
      okr_fit:
        label: "Alignment to current-year OKR or strategic initiative"
        scores:
          5: "Primary enabler of a Tier-1 OKR with documented linkage"
          4: "Supporting enabler of a Tier-1 OKR"
          3: "Aligned with a Tier-2 OKR"
          2: "Tangentially related to any OKR"
          1: "No discernible OKR connection"
      exec_sponsorship:
        label: "Executive sponsor tier and commitment level"
        scores:
          5: "C-suite sponsor, written commitment and budget line"
          4: "VP-level sponsor, written commitment"
          3: "Director sponsor, verbal commitment on record"
          2: "Team-lead champion only"
          1: "No sponsor identified"

  business_value:
    weight: 25
    max_raw: 5
    sub_criteria:
      roi_estimate:
        label: "Projected 3-year ROI (NPV, risk-adjusted)"
        scores:
          5: ">= $5M or >30% cost reduction"
          4: "$1M-$5M or 15-30% cost reduction"
          3: "$250K-$1M or 5-15% cost reduction"
          2: "$50K-$250K or <5% cost reduction"
          1: "No quantifiable financial benefit"
      customer_impact:
        label: "Direct improvement to internal or external customer experience"
        scores:
          5: "Affects all customers; measurable CSAT/NPS improvement expected"
          4: "Affects major customer segment"
          3: "Affects a defined internal team"
          2: "Convenience improvement only"
          1: "No customer impact"

  technical_feasibility:
    weight: 20
    max_raw: 5
    sub_criteria:
      data_readiness:
        label: "Availability and quality of required training/inference data"
        scores:
          5: "Production-grade labeled data available today"
          4: "Data exists; minor cleanup required (<4 weeks)"
          3: "Data partially available; 1-3 months to gather remainder"
          2: "Data exists but significant quality work required (3-6 months)"
          1: "Required data does not exist or is legally inaccessible"
      team_capability:
        label: "Team demonstrated capability for this class of AI system"
        scores:
          5: "Team has shipped identical system type in production"
          4: "Team shipped adjacent system; 1-2 skill gaps fillable within 4 weeks"
          3: "Team has 60%+ relevant skills; training plan defined"
          2: "Significant skill gaps; external hiring or partnership required"
          1: "No relevant AI delivery capability exists internally"

  risk_profile:
    weight: 15
    max_raw: 5
    # Inverse-scored: lower risk = higher contribution
    sub_criteria:
      regulatory_exposure:
        label: "Regulatory and compliance risk introduced"
        scores:
          5: "No new regulatory exposure"
          4: "Minor process controls needed; existing framework covers it"
          3: "Moderate — new internal policy needed; legal review required"
          2: "Significant — involves PII, financial model risk, or new data sharing"
          1: "High — requires regulator approval or novel legal interpretation"
      security_impact:
        label: "Cybersecurity and data-privacy attack surface expansion"
        scores:
          5: "No new attack surface introduced"
          4: "Minimal new surface; covered by existing security controls"
          3: "New surface area; standard remediation controls available"
          2: "Significant new surface; custom security controls required"
          1: "Critical — external-facing agentic execution or sensitive PII processing"

  urgency:
    weight: 15
    max_raw: 5
    sub_criteria:
      market_window:
        label: "Competitive or market time-sensitivity"
        scores:
          5: "Named competitor launched equivalent; existential catch-up required"
          4: "6-month window before competitive parity is lost"
          3: "12-month window; first-mover advantage at stake"
          2: "18-24 month window; moderate competitive advantage"
          1: "No discernible market window"
      regulatory_deadline:
        label: "Mandated compliance deadline driving this initiative"
        scores:
          5: "Hard legal deadline within 90 days"
          4: "Hard legal deadline within 6 months"
          3: "Anticipated regulatory deadline within 12 months"
          2: "Best-practice recommendation; no hard compliance date"
          1: "No regulatory driver"

thresholds:
  auto_approve:
    min_score: 80
    action: "Approved; added to portfolio backlog with P1 priority"
    notification_sla_days: 3
  committee_review:
    min_score: 60
    max_score: 79
    action: "Scheduled for next intake committee meeting"
    notification_sla_days: 5
  defer:
    min_score: 40
    max_score: 59
    action: "Placed on watchlist; re-evaluate in next quarterly cycle"
    notification_sla_days: 5
  reject:
    max_score: 39
    action: "Declined; requester notified with written rationale and score breakdown"
    notification_sla_days: 5

appeals:
  window_business_days: 10
  escalation_path:
    - intake_committee_chair
    - cto
  override_log_required: true
  override_alert_threshold_pct: 10  # alert if overrides exceed 10% of decisions
Examples

The following Python module implements the scoring data model and composite score calculation. Three independent reviewer score sets are aggregated via median to produce the final sub-criterion scores, which feed into the composite.
The following module illustrates Jira portfolio integration — creating an Epic from a scored proposal and tagging it with the decision band and composite score for portfolio view filtering.

# intake_portfolio_sync.py
# Syncs a scored intake proposal into the Jira portfolio as an Epic.

import os
import requests

JIRA_BASE  = os.environ["JIRA_BASE_URL"]       # https://acme.atlassian.net
JIRA_TOKEN = os.environ["JIRA_API_TOKEN"]
JIRA_EMAIL = os.environ["JIRA_USER_EMAIL"]
PROJECT    = "AIPORT"

BAND_LABELS = {
    "AUTO_APPROVE":     "intake-approved",
    "COMMITTEE_REVIEW": "intake-committee",
    "DEFER":            "intake-deferred",
    "REJECT":           "intake-rejected",
}

def sync_to_portfolio(proposal: dict) -> str:
    """Create or update a Jira Epic for an evaluated proposal."""
    score = proposal["composite_score"]
    band  = proposal["decision_band"]
    label = BAND_LABELS.get(band, "intake-unknown")

    body = {
        "fields": {
            "project":   {"key": PROJECT},
            "issuetype": {"name": "Epic"},
            "summary":   f"[AI Intake] {proposal['title']}",
            "description": {
                "version": 1, "type": "doc",
                "content": [{
                    "type": "paragraph",
                    "content": [{"type": "text", "text":
                        f"ID: {proposal['proposal_id']}\n"
                        f"Requester: {proposal['requester']}\n"
                        f"Composite Score: {score:.1f}/100\n"
                        f"Decision Band: {band}"
                    }]
                }]
            },
            "labels":   [label, f"score-{int(score)}"],
            "priority": {"name": "High" if score >= 80 else "Medium"},
        }
    }
    resp = requests.post(
        f"{JIRA_BASE}/rest/api/3/issue",
        json=body,
        auth=(JIRA_EMAIL, JIRA_TOKEN),
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        timeout=10,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["key"]
Real-World Example

A mid-sized financial-services firm — approximately 8,000 employees, with operations in retail banking, commercial lending, and wealth management — had been running ad-hoc AI initiative approvals for two years.
By the third year, the AI centre of excellence was receiving over forty proposal requests per quarter from fourteen business units.
Without a scoring framework, the CTO's team was making approval decisions in informal discussions, with no written rationale and no feedback to declined requesters.
Three business unit heads filed formal complaints alleging that their proposals had been bypassed in favour of technology-division initiatives without explanation.
The AI governance team ran a two-day rubric design workshop with representatives from each major business unit, the CFO's office, the CISO, and the chief risk officer.
They adopted the five-dimension framework with weights of 25 / 25 / 20 / 15 / 15 and wrote anchored descriptors for each sub-criterion.
A key design decision was the risk dimension: the CISO insisted that regulatory exposure and cybersecurity surface be
inverse-scored

— higher risk means a lower contribution — rather than simply excluded. This preserved the ability to approve high-risk initiatives with sufficient strategic and business value, while making the risk trade-off visible in the score.

The first live intake cycle processed forty-three proposals. Under the new rubric, eleven scored >=80 and were auto-approved, sixteen scored 60-79 and went to committee review (twelve approved, four deferred), fourteen scored 40-59 and were deferred, and two scored below 40 and were rejected.
Four requesters filed appeals. Two resulted in score adjustments that moved proposals from defer to committee review; both were ultimately approved.
The other two appeals were reviewed and the original scores confirmed — in both cases, the requesters acknowledged that the rubric correctly identified data readiness gaps they had not addressed.
After six quarterly cycles, the first major recalibration revealed that the urgency dimension's
market_window sub-criterion
was being systematically inflated — requesters had learned to frame nearly every initiative as having competitive time-sensitivity.
The rubric team sharpened the descriptors for the top two score points to require specific evidence of competitor activity (named competitor, product, launch date).
After the change, the average urgency dimension score dropped by 0.8 points across the cohort, reducing composite score inflation.
Common Mistakes
Publishing the rubric without a pilot.

Launching the first live intake cycle without scoring a historic cohort first guarantees calibration surprises. Always validate that rubric-derived decisions align with historical ones before going live.
Using mean instead of median aggregation.
Mean aggregation allows a single reviewer with a vested interest to pull a composite score above a threshold. Median is more resistant to outlier inflation.
Writing vague descriptors.

Text like 'high strategic value' or 'moderate risk' produces high inter-rater variance. Each descriptor must be specific enough that two independent reviewers reading it would assign the same score to the same proposal.
No appeals mechanism.
An intake scoring system without a credible, documented appeals path invites political escalation. Requesters need to know there is a legitimate channel to contest a score they believe is inaccurate.
Overrides becoming routine.
Executive overrides are legitimate for genuine strategic imperatives. When overrides occur on more than 10% of decisions, the rubric is no longer functioning as a governance mechanism.
Static weights over multi-year periods.
A rubric calibrated in 2023 may systematically mis-prioritise proposals in 2025 if the strategic context has shifted. Annual recalibration is mandatory, not optional.
Revealing individual reviewer scores.

Publishing which reviewer assigned which sub-criterion score exposes reviewers to social pressure from requesters and erodes scoring independence. Aggregated scores should be shared; individual reviewer scores remain confidential.
Treating the composite score as a continuous ranking.
A score of 73 vs 74 has no practical significance. Decision bands are the operative output; the composite score is a tool for reaching those bands, not a fine-grained ranking mechanism.
Best Practices
Version-control the rubric.
Store the YAML in git with semantic version tags. Each intake cycle should reference the rubric version in effect at cycle start. This enables retroactive audit and prevents mid-cycle rule changes.
Separate completeness review from scoring.
A dedicated completeness check prevents reviewers from spending scoring time on fundamentally incomplete submissions. Gate scoring on completeness.
Calibrate reviewers annually.

Run a calibration exercise using the same five benchmark proposals every year. Measure inter-rater agreement (Krippendorff's alpha or Cohen's kappa). If agreement falls below 0.6 for any dimension, that dimension's descriptors need sharpening.
Publish the full rubric, not just weights.
Requesters should see the complete descriptor anchors. Full transparency allows requesters to self-assess before submitting and reduces wasted proposal effort.
Log every override with structured metadata.
Override records should capture: proposal ID, original score, adjusted band, committee member requesting override, rationale category, and approving authority.
Provide score feedback within five business days.
Delayed feedback loses its value. Requesters who receive a score breakdown quickly can begin remediation work before the next intake cycle.
Maintain a public scoring archive.

A searchable record of all scored proposals — title, requester team, score, band, decision date — demonstrates fairness over time and allows new requesters to calibrate their proposals against past accepted ones.
Design for the appeal, not against it.
Treat every appeal as a signal that a descriptor or weight may need adjustment. Track appeals by dimension and use this data in the annual recalibration.
Performance and Scalability
Intake scoring systems face two distinct scalability challenges:
volume
(more proposals per cycle) and
organisational complexity
(more business units, regulatory jurisdictions, or product lines requiring customised sub-criteria).
For volume, the primary bottleneck is
reviewer time
. A five-reviewer panel scoring thirty proposals at one hour per proposal per reviewer requires 150 hours of senior staff time per quarter.
Organisations that exceed twenty proposals per cycle typically introduce a
two-stage triage
: a lightweight completeness and strategic-fit pre-screen (fifteen minutes per proposal) eliminates clearly non-viable proposals before they reach the full scoring panel.
Pre-screen typically removes 20-35% of submissions, substantially reducing the full scoring load.
For organisational complexity, the common pattern is a
hierarchical rubric
: a single corporate-level rubric for common dimensions (strategic alignment, risk, urgency) combined with domain-specific sub-criteria for business value and technical feasibility.
A retail banking division might have additional sub-criteria around credit model compliance and core banking system integration readiness that are irrelevant to a software engineering division.
The composite score formula must normalise across rubric variants to produce a comparable 0-100 scale across domains.
At scale, a spreadsheet-based evaluation system becomes operationally fragile beyond fifteen proposals per cycle.

The evaluation layer should be a purpose-built application or a ServiceNow PPM / Jira Portfolio plugin that enforces rubric structure, automates score aggregation, generates notification letters, and maintains the scoring archive.
Integration between the intake scoring tool and the portfolio management system should be real-time or near-real-time, with the portfolio record created automatically when a score is finalised.
Tooling and Ecosystem
The intake scoring ecosystem consists of four tool categories that must integrate coherently.
Proposal submission tools
— the front door through which requesters submit intake requests. ServiceNow Demand Management provides structured form fields, approval workflows, and native integration with portfolio management.
Jira Service Management offers similar capability with tighter integration into development workflows.
For organisations without enterprise PPM tools, a structured Google Form feeding a Google Sheet is a viable minimal option.
Scoring and aggregation tools
range from instrumented spreadsheets to purpose-built intake portal applications.

Enterprises with engineering capacity often build a lightweight Python or Node.js service that reads the YAML rubric, accepts reviewer score input via a REST API, performs median aggregation, and writes the composite score back to the portfolio system.
This approach gives the greatest flexibility for rubric evolution and integration but requires ongoing maintenance.
Portfolio management systems
— Jira Advanced Roadmaps, Microsoft Project for the Web, Planview Portfolios, and ServiceNow Strategic Portfolio Management — all support custom fields that store composite scores and decision bands.
Dashboards allow portfolio managers to filter the backlog by band, sort by composite score, and visualise score distributions across intake cycles.
Governance and audit tools
record the chain of evidence: which reviewer assigned which score, when the calibration session occurred, which overrides were applied and by whom, and which appeals were filed and decided.
Confluence, SharePoint, and Notion serve as governance wikis where the rubric, cycle records, and appeal outcomes are published.
For regulated industries, GRC platforms such as Archer or ServiceNow IRM provide additional audit trail capabilities with formal sign-off workflows.
Comparison with Alternatives
Method
How It Works
Strength
Weakness
Best Fit
Intake Scoring Criteria
Weighted multi-dimension rubric; composite 0-100 score
Transparent, consistent, auditable
Setup cost; gaming risk
Regulated enterprises; >15 proposals/cycle
First-Come-First-Served
Proposals admitted in submission order until capacity full
Zero process overhead
Ignores value and feasibility; rewards speed over quality
Very small portfolios or hackathon contexts
HiPPO (Highest-Paid Person's Opinion)
Senior executive makes approval decisions informally
Fast; full authority
Biased; non-reproducible; erodes team credibility
Turnaround situations requiring rapid executive action
Cost-Benefit Analysis Only
Rank proposals by estimated ROI; admit top N
Financial rigour; CFO-friendly
Ignores strategic fit, risk, feasibility; ROI estimates speculative at intake
Mature functions with reliable financial modelling
MoSCoW / Priority Tags
Proposals tagged Must/Should/Could/Won't by stakeholders
Simple; universally understood
Binary; subjective; no calibration across raters or business units
Agile teams with small, aligned stakeholder groups
ICE Scoring (Impact/Confidence/Ease)
Score each on three dimensions 1-10; rank by product
Lightweight; quick to calibrate
Not designed for multi-stakeholder enterprise contexts; lacks risk and strategic dimensions
Growth-stage product teams with limited governance overhead
Lean Portfolio / Portfolio Kanban
WIP limits enforce capacity discipline; ideas queue until capacity available
Flow-based; reduces batch overhead
No explicit priority signal — admission still a subjective decision
SAFe or LPM-aligned organisations with stable value stream priorities
Security and Reliability
Intake scoring systems handle proposal data that may be commercially sensitive — unreleased product strategies, financial projections, regulatory risk assessments, and competitive intelligence.
Access to the scoring tool should be restricted via
role-based access control (RBAC)

. Requesters can view their own proposal's score breakdown but not other proposals' scores. Portfolio managers and governance leads can view all records. Individual reviewer scores must be visible only to the committee chair and programme office.
The rubric YAML file is a governance artefact: unauthorised changes to dimension weights or descriptor thresholds could systematically bias scoring outcomes.
The rubric repository should require
pull request review by at least two committee members
before any change is merged, and every merge should be tagged with the effective date and linked to the governance record documenting the approved change.
Reliability considerations focus on
process continuity
. If the intake scoring tool is unavailable during an intake cycle, a documented fallback procedure — typically a shared scoring spreadsheet — must be available and tested.

Score data from each cycle should be exported to a durable archive immediately after the calibration session. This archive is the primary audit trail and must be retained for at least five years in most regulated industries.
Mental Model
Think of intake scoring criteria as a
loan underwriting system for AI project capital

. A bank does not lend money to every applicant who walks through the door. It applies a standardised credit scoring model that evaluates income, collateral, debt-to-income ratio, and credit history, each weighted by its predictive value for repayment.
Applications above a score threshold are approved automatically; those in the middle tier go to a loan officer for review; those below threshold are declined with a written explanation.
The applicant knows in advance what the criteria are, can prepare accordingly, and can appeal if they believe their score was calculated incorrectly.
The bank can defend every lending decision to regulators because the decision logic is documented, consistently applied, and produces a complete audit trail.
Intake scoring criteria apply exactly this discipline to the decision of which AI initiatives receive the organisation's scarce delivery capacity.
Key Takeaways
Five canonical dimensions.

Strategic alignment (25), business value (25), technical feasibility (20), risk profile (15), and urgency (15) cover the evaluation space for most enterprise AI initiatives. Adjust weights to reflect your current strategic context.
Composite score, four bands.
Auto-approve (>=80), committee review (60-79), defer (40-59), reject (<40). Bands provide decision clarity without false precision from a continuous ranking.
Anchored descriptors are the reliability mechanism.
Specific, observable descriptor text for each score point is what enables consistent scoring across independent reviewers. Vague descriptors are the primary source of inter-rater variance.
Median aggregation, not mean.
Aggregate multi-reviewer scores using the median to suppress outlier inflation from reviewers with vested interests in a proposal's outcome.
Version-control the rubric.
Treat the scoring YAML as code. Every weight or descriptor change must be reviewed, approved, and tagged with an effective date.
Build the appeals mechanism into the design.
A rubric without a credible, documented appeals path will face political escalation. Treat appeals data as calibration signal for the annual rubric review.
Log every override.
Executive overrides are legitimate but must be logged with structured metadata. Override rates above 10% signal that the rubric is no longer functioning as a governance control.
Recalibrate annually.
Run an inter-rater agreement analysis, review the score distribution, and analyse appeal outcomes after each year. Update descriptors or weights where the rubric is drifting from strategic reality.
Integrate with portfolio tooling.
The composite score and decision band should flow automatically into Jira, ServiceNow, or Azure DevOps to create the portfolio backlog record and trigger notifications.
Publish the full rubric to requesters.
Full transparency reduces gaming, improves proposal quality, and builds the credibility of the intake process as a fair and merit-based system.
